title: Security Policy Exception Register with Expiration Tracking

compliance.assurance_rationale: Uplifted from contributing to partial: UC provides meaningful monitoring evidence for CIP-003-8 R1 Part 1.2.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NERC CIP CIP-003-8 R1 Part 1.2 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.13.5: Security Policy Exception Register with Expiration Tracking.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.13.5' running on index=grc sourcetype=cip:policy_exception (exception_id, control_ref, expires_on, status), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

spl:
index=grc sourcetype=cip:policy_exception status="active" earliest=-120d
| eval exp_epoch=strptime(expires_on,"%Y-%m-%d")
| eval expired=if(now()>exp_epoch,1,0)
| eval days_left=round((exp_epoch-now())/86400,1)
| where expired=1 OR days_left<14
| table exception_id, control_ref, owner, expires_on, expired, days_left
description: We prevent policy exceptions from silently exceeding approved dates so security management controls stay within delegated risk acceptance.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for NERC CIP compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes grc and sourcetypes cip:policy_exception, cip:policy_exception`. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Ingest the exception register via HEC or DB Connect on change. (2) Map `control_ref` to CIP-003 policies. (3) Alert at fourteen days and on expiration. (4) Require closure events to flip status. Indexes required: `index=grc`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=cip:policy_exception`, `sourcetype=cip:policy_exception``.
visualization: Table (near-expired), Single value (expired count), Time chart (open exceptions)
knownFalsePositives: Administrative tasks, scheduled jobs or platform updates can match this pattern — correlate with change management, maintenance windows and user role before raising severity.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise Security (263).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=grc` `sourcetype=cip:policy_exception` (exception_id, control_ref, expires_on, status).
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Ingest the exception register via HEC or DB Connect on change. (2) Map `control_ref` to CIP-003 policies. (3) Alert at fourteen days and on expiration. (4) Require closure events to flip status.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=grc sourcetype=cip:policy_exception status="active" earliest=-120d
| eval exp_epoch=strptime(expires_on,"%Y-%m-%d")
| eval expired=if(now()>exp_epoch,1,0)
| eval days_left=round((exp_epoch-now())/86400,1)
| where expired=1 OR days_left<14
| table exception_id, control_ref, owner, expires_on, expired, days_left
```

Understanding this SPL

**Security Policy Exception Register with Expiration Tracking (CIP-003-8 R1 Part 1.2)** — We prevent policy exceptions from silently exceeding approved dates so security management controls stay within delegated risk acceptance.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=grc` `sourcetype=cip:policy_exception` (exception_id, control_ref, expires_on, status). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security (263). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: grc; **sourcetype**: cip:policy_exception. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=grc, sourcetype=cip:policy_exception, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **exp_epoch** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **expired** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **days_left** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
• Filters the current rows with `where expired=1 OR days_left<14` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.

Output fields: control_ref, days_left, exception_id, exp_epoch, expired, expires_on, owner.

Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (near-expired), Single value (expired count), Time chart (open exceptions)
grandmaExplanation: We watch for we prevent policy exceptions from silently exceeding approved dates so security management controls stay within delegated risk acceptance. This matters because it catches problems that could lead to audit findings or compliance gaps.
